title: Macramé garland
subtitle: A simple knotted cord garland with leaf and diamond motifs, approximately 120 cm long, for a shelf or mantelpiece
excerpt: A macramé garland is a short, decorative piece made from square knots and spiral knots arranged into repeating leaf and diamond shapes. This is an accessible first macramé project: it uses only two knot types, requires no frame or board, and produces a finished decorative piece in a few hours.
sourceNotes: Macramé garland pattern synthesised from general macramé knotting practice. Leaf and diamond motifs from square knots are a standard starting pattern for beginner macramé.

glossaryTerms.definition: The core macramé knot, formed by crossing the left working cord over two filler cords and through the right loop, then the right working cord over and through the left. Two square knots form one complete square knot unit.
A sequence of half-square knots (only the left-over-right half of a square knot) repeated without reversing direction. The consistent bias causes the working cords to spiral around the filler cords.
The folded-loop mounting knot used to attach cords to the dowel. Each cord folded in half and attached with a lark's head produces two working ends.
A macramé motif formed by arranging alternating square knots so that the open spaces between knot groups form diamond shapes visible against the knotted body.

content.text: A macramé garland is a forgiving first project: the pattern is made up of
along the length. There is no frame or board needed: the garland hangs from a short wooden dowel and the weight of the cords holds everything in place during knotting. All cords are mounted using

items.name: 3 mm natural or coloured cotton macramé cord

content.text: Step 1: Cut and mount the cords

Cut 12 lengths of cord at 200 cm each. Fold each length in half and attach to the dowel with a lark's head knot, spacing them evenly across the full 30 cm of the dowel. You now have 24 working ends. Hang the dowel from a hook at a comfortable working height so the cords hang freely below it.

Working from left to right, divide the 24 cords into 6 groups of 4. Tie 3 square knots per group, leaving 5 cm of cord above the first knot. Re-group: skip the first 2 cords, then take the next 4, and continue across, leaving the last 2 cords free. Tie 2 square knots in each new group of 4. The open spaces between groups form the first row of the diamond. Tie a third row of 3 square knots per group, back in the original grouping. The completed diamond shape is now visible.

Below the first diamond, tie 8 half-square knots (left over right only, not reversing) in each group of 4. The repeated one-sided knot causes the cord pairs to spiral gradually. These spiral sections add visual interest between the diamond motifs and are quick to produce. Continue alternating diamond motifs and spiral sections down the full length of the garland.

Trim the cord ends to an even length below the last section of knots, or cut them into a V-shape for a pointed finish. Unravel the cut ends by combing through them with a stiff brush or a wide comb to produce a soft fringe effect. Trim any uneven tails after brushing.

items.problem: The square knots are inconsistent in size across the garland
items.cause: The knot tightness varies between sessions or after resting
items.fix: Develop a consistent pulling habit: after each half of the square knot, pull both working cords to the same tension before continuing. Work at a comfortable arm height so the pull direction stays consistent.

items.problem: The spiral sections are loose and do not spiral clearly
items.cause: The half-square knots were not tied consistently on the same side
items.fix: Always begin each half-square knot with the same working cord (always left-over-right, or always right-over-left). Mark the starting cord with a piece of tape until the habit is established.

items.problem: The garland is significantly longer than planned
items.cause: The cord lengths were too generous or the diamond motifs were spaced further apart than intended
items.fix: Measure the garland after the first diamond and spiral section and compare with the planned length per repeat. Reduce the cord length by 20% for the next project.
